itunes_results.name: Calm
itunes_results.category: guided meditation and sleep app — focuses on relaxation and stress reduction; NOT the science of consciousness. Does not cover: why David Chalmers coined the term 'Hard Problem of Consciousness' in 1994 — the distinction between explaining brain function (easy problem) and explaining why there is subjective experience at all (the hard problem that no neuroscientist has solved), why Francis Crick and Christof Koch proposed in 1990 that consciousness is correlated with 40Hz gamma oscillations in the visual cortex — a theory that generated 30 years of debate about neural correlates of consciousness, or why Integrated Information Theory (IIT) by Giulio Tononi assigns a 'phi' value to any system — and if a system has phi > 0, it is conscious by definition, meaning a simple logic gate is technically slightly conscious.

itunes_results.name: Headspace
itunes_results.category: mindfulness training app — teaches present-moment awareness techniques; NOT consciousness research. Does not deliver: why the Global Workspace Theory (GWT) by Bernard Baars proposes consciousness is a 'workspace' that broadcasts information to specialized brain modules — explaining why we can only hold one conscious thought at a time while parallel processing millions of unconscious computations, why Antonio Damasio's somatic marker hypothesis argues consciousness requires a body — the feeling of the body's state is what creates the 'sense of self,' meaning consciousness cannot exist in a purely abstract AI, or why the binding problem in neuroscience has never been solved — when you see a red ball, color and shape are processed in separate brain regions, yet you experience them as unified. No theory explains the binding mechanism.

daily_consciousness_studies_streak.notes: No streak app delivers daily bite-sized lessons on consciousness science — IIT, GWT, neural correlates, the hard problem. Communities are massive and curious. App would teach: why you are conscious, what anesthesia actually does, whether AI could be conscious, and what psychedelics reveal about the default mode network.

itunes_results.name: Daily Stoic
itunes_results.category: Stoic philosophy daily app — focused exclusively on Stoicism; NOT philosophy of mind or consciousness debates. Does not cover: why Descartes' 'I think therefore I am' (1637) created the mind-body problem — his proposal that mind and body are distinct substances (dualism) created a question that philosophy has wrestled with for 400 years: how does an immaterial mind move a physical body, why Gilbert Ryle famously called Cartesian dualism the 'ghost in the machine' in 1949 — his argument that Descartes made a category mistake, treating the mind as a hidden theater-director inside the brain, or why John Searle's Chinese Room thought experiment (1980) argues that a computer that passes the Turing Test is still not conscious — it manipulates symbols without understanding their meaning, just as a person following translation rules in a room doesn't understand Chinese.

itunes_results.name: Philosophy Break
itunes_results.category: broad philosophy education app — covers many philosophy topics superficially; low review count indicates low traction. Does not deliver the depth needed: why functionalism (the view that mental states are defined by their causal roles, not their physical substrate) implies that sufficiently complex aliens or AIs could have minds — and why this view has been both liberating and terrifying for AI researchers since Turing, why eliminative materialism (Patricia Churchland, Paul Churchland) argues that folk psychology concepts like 'belief' and 'desire' will eventually be replaced by neuroscience — just as 'phlogiston' was replaced by oxidation, or why the explanatory gap between physical brain processes and subjective experience led philosopher Joseph Levine to argue in 1983 that even a complete neuroscience would leave something unexplained.

daily_philosophy_of_mind_streak.notes: No dedicated philosophy of mind streak app exists. 'Philosophy Break' has 820 reviews after years — essentially no market penetration. Core curriculum writes itself: dualism vs physicalism, functionalism, eliminativism, the hard problem, personal identity, free will. Each lesson is a famous thought experiment with modern scientific context.

itunes_results.name: BrainHQ
itunes_results.category: brain training games app — improves processing speed and memory through exercises; NOT cognitive bias education. Does not teach: why the availability heuristic (Kahneman & Tversky 1973) causes people to overestimate the likelihood of events that come easily to mind — explaining why people fear plane crashes more than car crashes despite car crashes being 95x more deadly, why the Dunning-Kruger effect (1999) showed that incompetent people systematically overestimate their competence — scoring in the 12th percentile on logical reasoning while estimating they scored in the 62nd percentile, or why loss aversion (Kahneman & Tversky 1979 prospect theory) means the pain of losing $100 is psychologically twice as powerful as the pleasure of gaining $100 — the finding that earned them the 2002 Nobel Prize in Economics.

itunes_results.name: Lumosity
itunes_results.category: brain training games — improves memory and processing speed through gamified tasks; NOT cognitive bias or decision-making education. Does not explain: why confirmation bias causes people to unconsciously seek information that confirms existing beliefs while discarding contradicting evidence — Peter Wason's 1960 card selection task showed that even highly intelligent people default to confirmation over falsification, why the sunk cost fallacy causes continued investment in failing ventures — the Vietnam War was extended partly because of 'we can't have died in vain,' or why the fundamental attribution error (Lee Ross 1977) causes people to overweight personality and underweight situational factors when explaining others' behavior — the finding that explains why we blame individuals for systemic failures.

daily_cognitive_bias_streak.notes: Daily cognitive bias lesson is a unique niche — no streak app teaches the 180+ documented cognitive biases with historical examples and modern research context. Users want this: they know they're biased but don't know what their biases are. App teaches bias, explains the psychology, gives a real-world example, provides a debiasing technique.

itunes_results.name: Waking Up
itunes_results.category: Sam Harris meditation and philosophy app — broad philosophy and mindfulness content; focuses on Harris' specific views. Does not deliver the full debate: why Benjamin Libet's 1983 experiments showed that brain activity predicting a hand movement begins 550ms before the subject consciously decides to move — suggesting the brain initiates action before consciousness is aware of the decision (though this interpretation is contested), why compatibilism (Hume, Kant, Dennett) argues free will and determinism are compatible — 'free will' just means acting from your own desires without external compulsion, not that your desires are uncaused, or why hard determinism (Schopenhauer, Laplace's demon) implies that every event including every human choice was fixed at the Big Bang — every murder, every kindness, every invention was inevitable the moment the universe began.

itunes_results.name: Great Courses Plus
itunes_results.category: lecture series subscription app — expensive ($250/yr), covers broad academic topics via long-form lectures; NOT a streak app. Does not provide bite-sized daily insights on: why quantum indeterminism (random quantum events in the brain proposed by Roger Penrose and Stuart Hameroff — Orchestrated Objective Reduction) was proposed as a physical basis for free will — and why neuroscientists overwhelmingly reject it as quantum effects at neural scale are swamped by thermal noise, why Peter Strawson's 1962 'Freedom and Resentment' argued that the free will debate is practically irrelevant — regardless of determinism, we cannot give up reactive attitudes like gratitude and indignation because they are constitutive of human relationships, or why Robert Sapolsky's 2023 book 'Determined' argues that neuroscience, genetics, and evolutionary biology together leave zero room for free will — and the implications for criminal justice if no one is truly responsible for their actions.

daily_free_will_debate_streak.notes: Free will vs determinism has enormous Reddit exposure (philosophy + atheism = 5.6M subscribers interested in this debate). No app delivers daily bite-sized lessons on the debate's key moments: Libet experiments, compatibilism arguments, neuroscience of volition. Waking Up is the closest competitor but it's Sam Harris' personal app, not an educational curriculum.

itunes_results.name: Eyecandy: Optical Illusion Lab
itunes_results.category: optical illusion viewer app — collection of visual illusions for entertainment; NOT neuroscience of perception. Does not explain: why the hollow face illusion works — the brain's prior expectation that faces are convex is so strong that it overrides the correct depth cue, causing a hollow face mask to appear as a protruding face even when you know it's hollow, why color constancy (Edwin Land's retinex theory) explains why a white shirt looks white under incandescent light and fluorescent light despite the physical wavelengths being completely different — the brain corrects for illuminant color, or why the McGurk effect (1976) shows that visual information overrides auditory information — when you see a mouth saying 'ga' while hearing 'ba,' you perceive 'da,' demonstrating that perception is multimodal fusion, not passive reception.

itunes_results.name: Illusions app
itunes_results.category: optical illusion collection — entertainment; NOT perception science education. Does not deliver: why the binding problem (how separate brain regions processing color, shape, motion, and depth are combined into a unified percept) remains unsolved in neuroscience — every proposed solution (temporal synchrony, attentional spotlight) has been experimentally challenged, why anosognosia (unawareness of one's own disability) — as seen in Anton syndrome where blind patients believe they can see — demonstrates that consciousness generates a model of reality, not reality itself, or why synesthesia (5 letters per 10,000 people experience it — perceiving letters as colored, music as shapes) reveals the cross-modal connectivity of perception and raises questions about what 'normal' perception means.

daily_perception_science_streak.notes: r/opticalillusions has 980K subscribers hungry for exactly this content. No app teaches the neuroscience behind perception, illusions, and reality construction. Massive educational market: why do we see what we see? Why are our senses unreliable? Each lesson is a famous illusion + the neuroscience + the philosophical implication about reality.

itunes_results.name: Shadow: A Dream Journal
itunes_results.category: dream journaling app — records and helps remember dreams; NOT dream science education. Does not teach: why REM sleep was discovered by Eugene Aserinsky and Nathaniel Kleitman in 1953 — observing that eyes move rapidly under closed lids during dreaming, and that this correlates with vivid dreaming, which overturned the view that the brain 'shuts off' during sleep, why Sigmund Freud's 'Interpretation of Dreams' (1900) proposed that dreams are disguised fulfillments of unconscious wishes — and why modern neuroscience (Hobson & McCarley's activation-synthesis hypothesis, 1977) replaced this with the view that dreams are the cortex's attempt to make meaning of random brainstem activations, or why lucid dreaming (knowing you are dreaming while in a dream) was scientifically verified in 1975 by Keith Hearne and later Keith LaBerge — using pre-agreed eye movement signals from sleeping subjects to prove awareness during REM.

itunes_results.name: Loóna: Sleepscape & Calm
itunes_results.category: sleep relaxation app — bedtime stories and visual relaxation to help fall asleep; NOT dream science. Does not explain: why sleep consolidation theory (Stickgold, Walker) shows that REM sleep is essential for procedural memory (motor skills, pattern recognition) — athletes who sleep 8+ hours perform measurably better than those who sleep 6 hours, why sleep paralysis — the inability to move while falling asleep or waking — is caused by muscle atonia persisting into wakefulness, and why it is often accompanied by hypnagogic hallucinations that explain historical accounts of 'demons sitting on the chest,' or why the default mode network (DMN) — the brain regions active during mind-wandering and daydreaming — was discovered by Marcus Raichle in 2001 and is now understood as central to self-referential thought, autobiographical memory, and theory of mind.

daily_dream_science_streak.notes: r/dreams (990K) and r/luciddreaming (490K) are dream-obsessed communities with no educational streak app serving them. Nobody teaches the actual science of dreams: sleep stages, why we dream, what lucid dreaming reveals, REM science. This app writes itself: daily lesson on the neuroscience + history of dream research.

itunes_results.name: Reflectly: AI Journal
itunes_results.category: AI-powered journaling and self-reflection app — focuses on emotional wellness and gratitude; NOT philosophy of personal identity. Does not explore: why the Ship of Theseus paradox — if a ship has every plank replaced, is it still the same ship — has been applied to human identity by Derek Parfit in 'Reasons and Persons' (1984): if every atom in your body is replaced over 7 years, are you the same person, why Parfit's teleportation thought experiment argues that what we care about in personal identity is psychological continuity (memories, personality, beliefs) not physical continuity — if your brain is scanned, destroyed, and perfectly reconstructed on Mars, the continuity of experience suggests it IS you, or why Buddhist philosophy (anatta — no-self) argues that the self is a useful fiction created by the mind, and why neuroscientist Anil Seth's 'controlled hallucination' model of consciousness agrees: the brain constructs a self-model that is not the true nature of the organism.

itunes_results.name: Who Am I — Your Brain on Self
itunes_results.category: psychology self-discovery app — brief interactive questions about personality; 210 reviews confirms minimal traction. Does not deliver: why John Locke's memory theory of personal identity (1689) argues that you are the same person across time if you can remember being that person — and why this has the absurd implication that a young soldier who remembers being flogged as a boy and an old general who remembers being the soldier are NOT the same person, why split-brain patients (whose corpus callosum has been severed to treat epilepsy) appear to have two separate streams of consciousness in one skull — the left hemisphere confabulates explanations for actions initiated by the right hemisphere, suggesting the unified self may be a narrative constructed after the fact, or why the continuity of consciousness during sleep is illusory — your experience of waking up as 'the same person' who fell asleep is a judgment made by the waking brain, not a direct experience of continuity.

daily_personal_identity_streak.notes: Personal identity is one of philosophy's most accessible entry points — everyone wonders 'who am I?' No streak app covers Parfit, Locke's memory theory, Buddhist no-self, split-brain research. This niche sits at the crossroads of philosophy, neuroscience, and spiritual practice — huge addressable audience.

itunes_results.name: Daily Stoic App
itunes_results.category: Stoicism quotes and reflection app by Ryan Holiday — daily Stoic quotes with brief commentary; LIMITED depth, no historical context, no rival schools comparison. Does not cover: why the three disciplines of Stoicism (Epictetus: the discipline of desire, action, and assent) form a complete psychological system for eliminating suffering — and why each discipline maps onto a specific cognitive practice that modern CBT independently rediscovered 2,000 years later, why Marcus Aurelius wrote the Meditations (167-180 AD) as private notes to himself, never intended for publication — making it the only journal of a Roman Emperor, written during plague and war on the Danube frontier, revealing Stoic practice as daily struggle not natural virtue, or why Chrysippus of Soli (279-206 BC) systematized Stoicism into formal logic — arguing that the Stoic sage (sophos) is an almost impossible ideal, and that 99.99% of people are 'fools' (phauloi) in Stoic terms, making the philosophy aspirational rather than descriptive.

itunes_results.name: Stoic: Daily Journal + Quotes
itunes_results.category: Stoic journaling app — morning and evening reflection with Stoic exercises; strong but generic. Does not differentiate on: why the Stoic cosmology — the universe as a living organism guided by Logos (divine reason) — was the metaphysical foundation for all Stoic ethics, and why without this cosmology the ethics become untethered from their original justification, why the contrast between Stoicism and Epicureanism was the defining intellectual debate of ancient Rome — Epicurus said pursue pleasure (ataraxia through withdrawal), Zeno said pursue virtue (eudaimonia through engagement) — and why nearly every Roman intellectual took a position in this debate, or why Stoicism's revival in the 20th century came through Viktor Frankl's logotherapy and Albert Ellis's Rational Emotive Behavior Therapy — both explicitly citing Epictetus' Enchiridion as the philosophical foundation.

daily_stoic_philosophy_streak.notes: Stoicism has two apps with real traction (Daily Stoic: 9.8K reviews, Stoic Journal: 31K reviews). Market is NOT empty but IS large enough for differentiation. A historically-grounded Stoicism app teaching the cosmology, the rival schools, CBT parallels, and complete philosophical system would be differentiated. SOFT_COMPETITION not BLUE_OCEAN.

itunes_results.name: Waking Up
itunes_results.category: Sam Harris meditation and philosophy app — personal perspective on consciousness and meditation; NOT scientific survey of altered states research. Does not cover: why psilocybin reduces activity in the default mode network (DMN) — the brain's 'self-narrative' system — and why this correlates with mystical experiences and ego dissolution, as shown in Robin Carhart-Harris' 2012 Imperial College study, why sensory deprivation (flotation tanks, first built by John Lilly in 1954) produces reliable altered states including hallucinations and temporal distortion without any drug — because the brain, lacking external input, amplifies internal signal, or why the Ganzfeld effect (uniform visual field) reliably produces hallucinations within 30 minutes in normal subjects — the perceptual system, starved of change, generates its own content, explaining how mystics in featureless desert environments reported visions.

itunes_results.name: Insight Timer
itunes_results.category: meditation and mindfulness app — largest meditation library; NOT altered states science. Does not teach: why holotropic breathing (Stanislav Grof) can produce LSD-like experiences through hyperventilation — the CO2 reduction causes cerebral vasoconstriction and altered brain states without any chemical, why the binding effect between 5-HT2A serotonin receptor activation and psychedelic effects was discovered by David Nichols — establishing that all classic psychedelics work through the same receptor mechanism and explaining cross-tolerance, or why near-death experiences (NDEs) share universal features across cultures (tunnel, light, life review, border) — with neuroscience offering three competing explanations: REM intrusion, dying brain serotonin flood, or endogenous DMT release.

daily_altered_states_streak.notes: Altered states research is exploding post-psychedelic renaissance. No streak app teaches the neuroscience: psilocybin, DMT, NDE research, sensory deprivation science, holotropic breathing. Communities are hungry for credible science-based information. Ideal app: daily lesson on the neuroscience and history of altered states research with zero drug promotion.

itunes_results.name: Noom
itunes_results.category: psychology-based weight loss app — uses CBT and behavioral change for weight management; NOT mind-body medicine science. Does not teach: why Herbert Benson's 'relaxation response' (1975 Harvard study) showed that meditation produces measurable physiological changes — reduction in heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen consumption, and cortisol — the first scientific evidence that the mind directly affects body physiology, why the placebo effect is not 'just in your head' — Fabrizio Benedetti's research showed placebos cause real neurobiological changes (endorphin release, dopamine shifts) and that open-label placebos (telling patients 'this is a sugar pill') still work — suggesting conscious belief alone activates healing mechanisms, or why psychoneuroimmunology (PNI — the study of how the mind affects the immune system) began with Robert Ader's 1975 discovery that rats could be conditioned to suppress their immune systems — proving that the nervous system and immune system communicate directly.

itunes_results.name: Stress & Anxiety Companion
itunes_results.category: anxiety management app — breathing exercises and cognitive techniques for stress; NOT mind-body medicine science. Does not deliver: why epigenetics research (Moshe Szyf, Michael Meaney) showed that childhood trauma creates heritable gene expression changes — and that meditation can reverse some of these epigenetic markers, why the vagus nerve (the main parasympathetic highway from brain to gut, heart, and lungs) explains why breathing exercises reduce anxiety — slow exhalation activates vagal brake, and why vagal tone (measured by heart rate variability) predicts both physical and mental health outcomes, or why the gut microbiome influences mental health — the gut-brain axis communicates via the vagus nerve, and gut bacteria produce 95% of the body's serotonin, explaining why antibiotic courses often cause temporary mood changes and why probiotic research is now mainstream psychiatry.

daily_mind_body_science_streak.notes: Mind-body science is validated by mainstream research but no streak app teaches it. Placebo research, psychoneuroimmunology, relaxation response, vagal tone, gut-brain axis — all documented, fascinating, actionable. Noom (540K reviews) occupies the behavioral weight loss niche but not the science education niche. Clean opening.
